Title: Hyperosmotic stress-induced microtubule disassembly in Chlamydomonas reinhardtii

Microbial_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: The PHS1 gene is single-copy in Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, located on chromosome 8 with 17 exons and 16 introns, and its intron positions are supported by RNA-seq analysis. Genetic and biochemical data indicate that PHS1 is solely responsible for hyperosmotic stress-induced α-tubulin Thr349 phosphorylation in C. reinhardtii. Comparative genomics shows that PHS1 homologues with similar domain architecture (tubulin kinase plus MAP kinase phosphatase-like domain) are present in many freshwater Chlorophyta but absent from some fully marine green algae, suggesting an evolutionary association with freshwater or non-saline habitats.
Microbial_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. Chlamydomonas reinhardtii possesses a single-copy PHS1 gene (Cre08.g375000) encoding a protein with an N-terminal tubulin kinase domain and a C-terminal MAP kinase phosphatase-like domain.
2. Mild hyperosmotic stress (e.g., 0.2–0.3 M sorbitol or ~0.15 M NaCl, 20–40% artificial seawater) rapidly and transiently induces PHS1-dependent phosphorylation of α-tubulin at Thr349, with more than 60% of cellular tubulin phosphorylated at peak.
3. In wild-type and PHS1-complemented cells, PHS1-mediated tubulin phosphorylation causes rapid, transient depolymerization of cytoplasmic microtubules in both G1 and mitotic cells, with microtubule arrays recovering within about 1 h in G1 and within several hours in mitosis.
4. The phs1 insertion mutant completely lacks stress-induced α-tubulin Thr349 phosphorylation and fails to undergo rapid microtubule depolymerization under hyperosmotic stress, confirming that PHS1 is solely responsible for this modification.
5. Transient microtubule destabilization mediated by PHS1 does not measurably affect G1 phase cell growth (cell volume increase) under sorbitol stress but contributes to delaying mitotic progression and daughter cell cluster formation under 0.3 M sorbitol.
6. Hyperosmotic stress strongly inhibits daughter cell hatching, causing persistent daughter cell clusters (palmelloid-like aggregates); this mitotic delay is less severe in phs1 cells than in wild type and complemented lines, indicating that PHS1-mediated microtubule destabilization is partly responsible for stress-responsive cell cycle delay.
7. PHS1 homologues with conserved tubulin kinase and phosphatase domain architecture are widely present in freshwater Chlorophyta but absent in some marine green algae, supporting a role for PHS1-mediated cytoskeletal remodeling in adaptation to freshwater or moderately saline environments.
Microbial_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PHS1 mediates hyperosmotic stress-induced phosphorylation of α-tubulin at Thr349, causing transient depolymerization of cytoplasmic microtubules and contributing to a delay in mitotic cell cycle progression under mild hyperosmotic stress, while having little effect on G1 phase cell growth
Microbial_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: Upon mild hyperosmotic stress, PHS1 tubulin kinase activity is rapidly activated and phosphorylates α-tubulin at Thr349, reducing tubulin polymerization competence and causing transient depolymerization of cytoplasmic microtubules; phosphorylated tubulin is then dephosphorylated within 1 h in G1 cells and within ~3 h in mitotic cells, restoring microtubule organization. This transient microtubule destabilization contributes to delaying mitotic progression under stress.
Microbial_Genes.Regulatory_Pathway: Hyperosmotic stress response pathway leading to rapid cytoskeletal remodeling (microtubule destabilization) and stress-responsive regulation of mitotic cell cycle progression in freshwater green algae
Microbial_Genes.Upstream_or_Downstream_Regulation: PHS1 --(protein kinase phosphorylation of microtubule subunit under hyperosmotic stress)--> α-tubulin (Thr349) -->(reduced polymerization)--> transient depolymerization of cytoplasmic microtubules -->(contributes to)--> delay in mitotic progression under hyperosmotic stress.
Microbial_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PHS1 encodes a large protein with an N-terminal atypical tubulin kinase domain (related to actin-fragmin kinases) and a C-terminal MAP kinase phosphatase-like domain within a single polypeptide
Microbial_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mild hyperosmotic stress imposed by 0.2–0.3 M sorbitol, 0.1–0.2 M NaCl, or 20–40% artificial seawater, applied to synchronized cells in G1 phase or just before/during mitosis
Microbial_Genes.Research_Topic: Hyperosmotic and salinity stress responses, cytoskeletal remodeling, and stress-responsive cell cycle regulation in freshwater green algae
Microbial_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress response (hyperosmotic and salinity stress) and cell cycle/mitotic progression via microtubule cytoskeleton dynamics
Microbial_Genes.Quantitative_Phenotypic_Alterations: Under 0.3 M sorbitol, more than 60% of cellular tubulin becomes phosphorylated at Thr349 at peak response (10 min in G1, 30 min in mitosis). In wild-type and complemented cells, hyperosmotic stress causes a strong, statistically significant reduction in interphase microtubule occupancy after 10 min, which recovers to near control levels within 1 h, whereas phs1 mutants maintain higher microtubule polymer amounts. Hyperosmotic 0.3 M sorbitol treatment at 9 h delays formation of daughter cell clusters and completely prevents release of free daughter cells up to 12 h in all genotypes, but mitotic progression (cluster formation) is significantly less delayed in phs1 cells compared with wild type and complemented lines.
Microbial_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: Hyperosmotic stress inhibits daughter cell hatching and promotes prolonged retention of newly divided cells in clusters (palmelloid-like aggregates); this stress-triggered delay in mitotic progression and daughter cell cluster formation is less pronounced in phs1 mutant cells than in wild type or complemented lines, indicating that PHS1 partly mediates this response
Microbial_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Chlamydomonas reinhardtii CC-4533 wild type; PHS1 insertion mutant LMJ.RY0402.181368 (phs1-1); PHS1-complemented line; additional C. reinhardtii strains C9, CC-125, CC-5155, CC-5325 for cell-wall status tests
Microbial_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Cell cycle synchronization in 12 h light/12 h dark; hyperosmotic treatments with artificial seawater, sorbitol, and NaCl; immunoblotting with phospho-Thr349-α-tubulin-specific and total α-tubulin antibodies; phos-tag SDS-PAGE to quantify phosphorylated versus non-phosphorylated tubulin; construction and analysis of PHS1 insertion mutant and genomic complementation line; immunofluorescence microscopy of microtubules and acetylated microtubules; cell size and number measurements; mitotic progression and daughter cell cluster formation assays under stress
Microbial_Genes.Strain_or_Isolate: CC-4533 wild type, PHS1 insertion mutant LMJ.RY0402.181368 (phs1-1), complemented PHS1 line; additional strains C9, CC-125, CC-5155, CC-5325 used for stress-induced tubulin phosphorylation assays
Microbial_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function analysis using a PHS1 insertion mutant (phs1-1) and genomic complementation, combined with stress-induced tubulin phosphorylation assays (phospho-Thr349-α-tubulin immunoblotting and phos-tag SDS-PAGE) and microtubule immunofluorescence to assess microtubule depolymerization and mitotic progression under hyperosmotic stress
Microbial_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Homology-based identification of a single-copy PHS1 gene (Cre08.g375000) on chromosome 8, followed by functional analysis using a PHS1 insertion mutant (aphVIII insertion) and genomic complementation with an 11,063 bp PHS1 genomic fragment
Microbial_Genes.Application_Value: PHS1 defines an algal-specific mechanism linking hyperosmotic stress sensing to rapid microtubule depolymerization and mitotic delay, suggesting it as a potential target to modulate stress tolerance, division timing, and aggregation behavior (palmelloid formation) in freshwater green algae. Understanding PHS1 function and its presence across freshwater Chlorophyta provides a basis for engineering or selecting algal strains with tailored responses to osmotic and salinity fluctuations in natural ecosystems and biotechnological cultivation systems.
